items.subtopic: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging: Echocardiography, Nuclear Cardiology, and Magnetic Resonance/Computed Tomography Imaging
items.title: Why Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging is Crucial in Cardiovascular Diagnosis
items.content: Noninvasive cardiac imaging has revolutionized cardiovascular medicine by providing detailed insights into heart and vessel structures without invasive procedures. It complements traditional diagnostic methods such as history taking, physical examination, blood tests, and exercise testing.
keyPoints: Imaging modalities include echocardiography, nuclear scintigraphy (including PET), MRI, and CT.
These techniques can be used independently or in conjunction with stress testing for diagnosis or prognosis.

items.subtopic: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging: Echocardiography, Nuclear Cardiology, and Magnetic Resonance/Computed Tomography Imaging
items.title: How Modern Cardiovascular Imaging Modalities Complement Each Other
items.content: Modern cardiovascular imaging modalities provide complementary information that enhances the diagnosis and management of cardiovascular diseases. Each modality has unique benefits and can be used in combination with stress testing to improve diagnostic accuracy.
keyPoints: Echocardiography (cardiac ultrasound) is a key noninvasive tool for visualizing heart structure and function.
Nuclear scintigraphy, including PET imaging, assesses myocardial perfusion and viability.
MRI offers detailed anatomical and functional information without ionizing radiation.
CT provides high-resolution images of coronary arteries and can be used to assess calcification.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging: Echocardiography, Nuclear Cardiology, and Magnetic Resonance/Computed Tomography Imaging

items.title: Understanding Doppler Ultrasound for Blood Flow Assessment
items.content: Doppler ultrasound is used to measure blood flow velocity and pressure gradients within the heart. It relies on the Doppler shift principle, where the frequency of reflected ultrasound changes based on the movement of red blood cells relative to the transducer.
keyPoints: The modified Bernoulli equation (p=4ν²) calculates the pressure gradient from blood flow velocity.
Three types of Doppler are used: spectral Doppler (including pulsed and continuous wave), and color flow Doppler.
Spectral Doppler displays a waveform with time on the horizontal axis and velocity on the vertical axis.
Pulsed wave Doppler assesses low-velocity flow at specific locations, while continuous wave Doppler measures high-velocity flow but lacks depth specificity.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (part 1)

items.title: Advantages and Limitations of High-Frequency Ultrasound
items.content: High-frequency ultrasound provides better spatial resolution, allowing for detailed imaging of small structures. However, it has reduced penetration depth, making it suitable for specific applications like pediatric or transesophageal echocardiography.
keyPoints: Higher frequency ultrasound offers greater spatial resolution but decreased penetration.
Used in pediatric imaging and transesophageal echocardiography due to proximity of the transducer to cardiac structures.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (part 1)

items.title: Three-Dimensional Echocardiography for Cardiac Assessment
items.content: Three-dimensional echocardiography uses a matrix array transducer to create pyramidal data sectors, enhancing the assessment of congenital heart disease and valve structures.
keyPoints: 3D ultrasound is increasingly used in transesophageal echocardiography for detailed cardiac assessments.
Current image quality with 3D echocardiography lags behind that of 2D ultrasound.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (part 1)

items.title: When to Use Transesophageal Echocardiography
items.content: Transesophageal echocardiography is preferred for detailed assessment of cardiac structures that are challenging to visualize with standard transthoracic imaging, such as small lesions including valvular vegetations and intracardiac thrombi. It provides higher image quality due to the use of a high-frequency probe and requires both topical and systemic anesthesia.
keyPoints: Higher spatial resolution than transthoracic echocardiography
Test of choice for small lesions, valvular vegetations, intracardiac thrombi
Requires conscious sedation and carries risks like esophageal damage or perforation
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (part 2)

items.title: Advantages of Stress Echocardiography
items.content: Stress echocardiography is used to evaluate cardiac function under exercise conditions, identifying myocardial ischemia or assessing valvular function. It can be performed with physical exertion or pharmacologic stress using dobutamine.
keyPoints: Assesses cardiac function during exercise
Identifies myocardial ischemia and evaluates valvular function
Can use treadmill/bicycle exercise or pharmacologic stress (dobutamine)
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (part 2)

items.title: Why Use Radionuclide Imaging for CAD?
items.content: Radionuclide imaging is essential in evaluating patients with known or suspected coronary artery disease (CAD) due to its ability to assess myocardial viability and stratify risk. This technique involves the use of radiopharmaceuticals that emit gamma rays, captured by specialized scanners like SPECT and PET, to create detailed images of the heart.
keyPoints: Used for initial diagnosis and risk stratification in CAD
Assesses myocardial viability
Involves intravenous injection of radiopharmaceuticals
Gamma rays emitted are captured by SPECT or PET scanners
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 1)

items.title: When to Choose Pharmacologic Stress Over Exercise in Myocardial Perfusion Imaging?
items.content: Pharmacologic stress is preferred over exercise for myocardial perfusion imaging in patients unable to perform adequate physical exertion. Coronary vasodilators like adenosine, dipyridamole, or regadenoson are commonly used, while dobutamine is an alternative for those with chronic pulmonary disease.
keyPoints: Pharmacologic stress as alternative when exercise isn't possible
Coronary vasodilators and $\beta_{1}$-receptor agonists like dobutamine used
Dobutamine preferred in patients with chronic pulmonary issues
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 1)

items.title: Advantages of PET Over SPECT in Myocardial Perfusion Imaging?
items.content: PET myocardial perfusion imaging offers improved diagnostic accuracy and lower radiation doses compared to SPECT. It provides absolute measures of myocardial perfusion, which enhances the assessment of regional and global coronary flow reserve, particularly beneficial for obese patients, women, and those with diabetes.
keyPoints: Improved diagnostic accuracy over SPECT
Lower radiation dose
Provides absolute measures of myocardial perfusion (mL/min per gram)
Enhances assessment in specific populations like obese patients and diabetics
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 1)

items.title: Myocardial Viability and Inflammation Assessment with Radionuclide Imaging
items.content: For evaluating myocardial viability in ischemic cardiomyopathy, FDG-PET combined with SPECT is preferred. Thallium-201 SPECT serves as an alternative when PET is unavailable. FDG-PET also aids in assessing myocardial and vascular inflammation and suspected infective endocarditis.
keyPoints: FDG-PET combined with SPECT for myocardial viability
Thallium-201 SPECT as an alternative to PET
FDG-PET used for myocardial/vascular inflammation and infective endocarditis
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 2) | CARDIAC COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY

items.title: Diagnosing Cardiac Amyloidosis with Bone Scintigraphy
items.content: Bone scintigraphy using SPECT and $^{99m}$Tc radiotracers is employed to diagnose transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis accurately.
keyPoints: Use of bone-seeking $^{99m}$Tc radiotracers for diagnosing transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 2) | CARDIAC COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY

items.title: CT Calcium Scoring in Assessing Atherosclerosis Risk
items.content: CT calcium scoring quantifies coronary artery calcification, associated with atherosclerosis burden and cardiovascular mortality. Scores are categorized as minimal (0–10), mild (10–100), moderate (100–400), or severe (>400) and normalized by age and gender.
keyPoints: Coronary calcium scoring categories: minimal (0–10), mild (10–100), moderate (100–400), severe (>400)
CAC scores normalized by age and gender
Low radiation dose (~1–2 mSv) for CAC scanning
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIONUCLIDE IMAGING (part 2) | CARDIAC COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY

items.title: Why Use T1 and T2 Mapping in Cardiac MRI?
items.content: Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging utilizes T1 and T2 mapping to quantify myocardial tissue characteristics. T1 mapping is primarily used for assessing the spectrum of myocardial inflammation or fibrosis, while T2 mapping is employed to evaluate myocardial edema.
keyPoints: T1-weighted pulse sequences assess cardiac structure and function, blood flow, and myocardial perfusion with pharmacologic stress.
T2-weighted pulse sequences evaluate myocardial edema and myocardial iron infiltration.
T1 mapping is commonly used to scale the spectrum of myocardial inflammation or fibrosis.
T2 mapping is used for assessing myocardial edema.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC MAGNETIC RESONANCE

items.title: Indications and Advancements in Cardiac MRI Techniques
items.content: CMR is indicated for evaluating the etiology of cardiomyopathy, myocardial ischemia from chest pain syndromes, myocardial viability for revascularization benefits, pericardial diseases, and myocardial substrates of arrhythmias. Recent technical advancements have eliminated the need for ECG gating and breath-holding by using rapid data collection algorithms and diaphragmatic position gating.
keyPoints: Common indications for CMR include assessing cardiomyopathy etiology, myocardial ischemia, myocardial viability, pericardial diseases, and arrhythmias substrates.
Vector ECG-gating and repetitive patient breath-holding traditionally suppress cardiac and respiratory motions.
Technical advancements have eliminated the need for ECG gating and breath-holding in challenging situations.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC MAGNETIC RESONANCE

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: Echocardiography as Primary Imaging for Cardiac Structure Assessment
items.content: Echocardiography is the primary imaging method used to assess cardiac structure and function, including chamber size and ventricular wall thickness. It provides valuable insights into both cardiac and systemic diseases.
keyPoints: Primary imaging method for assessing cardiac structure and function.
Assesses size of all four chambers and thickness of ventricular walls.
Affected by both cardiac and systemic diseases.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: Accuracy of 3D Echocardiography in Volume Quantification
items.content: Three-dimensional (3D) echocardiography does not require geometric assumptions for quantifying left ventricular volumes and ejection fraction, offering more accurate assessments compared to traditional methods.
keyPoints: Does not require geometric assumptions for volume quantification.
More accurate than 2D echocardiography in certain contexts.
Requires substantial expertise; not widely used.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: Role of CMR in Determining Cardiomegaly Etiology
items.content: Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance (CMR) is particularly useful for determining the etiology of cardiomegaly and ventricular dysfunction, with late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) being nearly pathognomonic for myocardial infarction.
keyPoints: Useful in determining etiology of cardiomegaly and ventricular dysfunction.
Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) is nearly pathognomonic for myocardial infarction.
Can assess regional perfusion or metabolic defects.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR SYSTOLIC FUNCTION
items.title: Ejection Fraction as a Primary Measure for Systolic Function Assessment
items.content: The ejection fraction (EF) is the primary method to evaluate left ventricular systolic function, calculated by subtracting end-systolic volume from end-diastolic volume and dividing by end-diastolic volume. While all imaging modalities can measure EF, tomographic techniques like CMR, CT, and radionuclide imaging are more accurate than echocardiography due to their independence from geometric assumptions and operator skill.
keyPoints: Ejection fraction (EF) is calculated as: (End-Diastolic Volume - End-Systolic Volume) / End-Diastolic Volume
A LVEF of 55% or greater is generally considered normal; 50-55% is low-normal.
Tomographic techniques are more accurate and reproducible than echocardiography for EF measurement.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR SYSTOLIC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR SYSTOLIC FUNCTION
items.title: Advanced Techniques in Systolic Function Assessment: Myocardial Strain Imaging
items.content: Newer methods like myocardial strain or deformation imaging provide a more sensitive approach to detecting systolic dysfunction. These techniques, including speckle-tracking on echocardiography and feature tracking on CMR, are geometric assumption-independent and useful in conditions such as valvular heart disease and cardiotoxicity detection.
keyPoints: Myocardial strain imaging is geometric assumption-independent and more sensitive for detecting systolic dysfunction.
Strain patterns can vary by disease; e.g., cardiac amyloidosis often shows reduced myocardial strain at the heart's base with apex sparing.
Strain imaging aids in early detection of cardiotoxicity from chemotherapy/radiation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR SYSTOLIC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR DIASTOLIC FUNCTION
items.title: Understanding the Prognostic Value of E' in Diastolic Dysfunction
items.content: Echocardiography, with its high temporal resolution, is pivotal for assessing diastolic function. Recent advancements allow for precise measurement of myocardial wall motion velocity via mitral annular relaxation velocity (E'). E' inversely correlates with the time constant of relaxation (tau) and holds prognostic significance in heart failure patients.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is the primary method for assessing diastolic function due to high temporal resolution.
Mitral annular relaxation velocity (E') is inversely related to the time constant of relaxation (tau).
E' has prognostic significance in heart failure patients.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR DIASTOLIC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR DIASTOLIC FUNCTION
items.title: Using E/E' Ratio to Correlate with Left Ventricular Filling Pressures
items.content: The ratio of standard mitral inflow maximal velocity (E) to mitral annular relaxation velocity (E') is a critical measure. This E/E' ratio correlates with left ventricular filling pressures, providing valuable insights into diastolic function.
keyPoints: Dividing the standard mitral inflow maximal velocity (E) by the mitral annular relaxation velocity (E') yields E/E'.
E/E' correlates with left ventricular filling pressures.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF LEFT VENTRICULAR DIASTOLIC FUNCTION

items.title: Importance of Right Ventricular Function Assessment
items.content: Right ventricular size and function are prognostically important in various conditions. CMR is the most accurate noninvasive technique for evaluating right ventricular structure and ejection fraction.
keyPoints: CMR is considered the most accurate method to evaluate right ventricular structure and ejection fraction.
Echocardiography often provides qualitative assessments due to the right ventricle's unusual geometry, but quantitative methods like FAC and TAPSE are available.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF RIGHT VENTRICULAR FUNCTION

items.title: Right Ventricular Dysfunction in Acute Pulmonary Embolism
items.content: Acute pulmonary embolism can cause profound right ventricular dilatation and dysfunction, indicating significant hemodynamic compromise. The McConnell sign is a specific pattern of regional dysfunction associated with acute pulmonary embolism.
keyPoints: Right ventricular dilatation and dysfunction in acute pulmonary embolism indicate substantial hemodynamic compromise.
The McConnell sign (preservation of basal and apical wall motion with mid free wall dyskinesis) is highly specific for acute pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF RIGHT VENTRICULAR FUNCTION

items.title: Right Ventricular Dilatation Causes and Implications
items.content: Right ventricular dilatation can result from intrinsic diseases, increased pulmonary vascular resistance, or left-sided heart disease. It is associated with worse outcomes when due to predominantly left-sided disease.
keyPoints: Intrinsic diseases like congenital abnormalities and right ventricular infarction affect the right ventricle directly.
Increased pulmonary vascular resistance from conditions like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease can lead to cor pulmonale.
Right ventricular dilatation due to left-sided heart disease is associated with worse outcomes.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSMENT OF RIGHT VENTRICULAR FUNCTION

items.title: Understanding Radiation Doses from Cardiac Imaging Modalities
items.content: Cardiac imaging modalities such as myocardial perfusion SPECT, PET scans, and cardiac CT expose patients to ionizing radiation. The effective dose varies based on the protocol and technology used. Newer technologies in cardiac CT have significantly reduced radiation doses.
keyPoints: Effective dose for myocardial perfusion SPECT scan: ~4–11 mSv
Effective dose for myocardial perfusion PET scan: ~2.0–4 mSv
Average radiation dose for coronary CTA: 3 to 15 mSv, can be as low as 1 mSv with newer technologies
Radiation exposure from natural sources in the U.S.: ~3 mSv annually
ALARA principle emphasizes minimizing radiation while achieving clinical objectives
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIATION EXPOSURE

items.title: Balancing Risks and Benefits of Radiation Exposure in Cardiac Imaging
items.content: The risk of fatal malignancy from imaging-related radiation is small but must be weighed against the benefits of obtaining critical diagnostic information. Clinicians should ensure that tests are appropriate, necessary, and likely to influence clinical management before proceeding.
keyPoints: Risk of fatal malignancy from medical imaging-related radiation: difficult to estimate precisely
Average dose for invasive coronary angiography: ~7 mSv
Importance of assessing risk-versus-benefit ratio in individual patients
Avoid routine follow-up scans in asymptomatic individuals unless clinically indicated
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · RADIATION EXPOSURE

items.title: Understanding the Risks of Iodinated Contrast Agents in Cardiac CT
items.content: Iodinated contrast agents enhance cardiac CT imaging but carry risks, including adverse reactions and contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN). The incidence of contrast reactions ranges from 0.4% to 3%, with nonionic formulations being safer than ionic ones. CIN is rare in patients with normal renal function (eGFR >60 mL/min) but poses a higher risk for those with impaired renal function, particularly older diabetic individuals.
keyPoints: Incidence of contrast reactions: 0.4–3% with nonionic formulations
CIN risk is low in patients with eGFR >60 mL/min
Increased CIN risk in patients with GFR <60 mL/min, especially older diabetics
Pre- and postscan hydration recommended for high-risk patients
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CONTRAST AGENTS

items.title: Safety Profile of Gadolinium-Based Contrast Agents (GBCAs) in CMR Imaging
items.content: Gadolinium-based contrast agents enhance myocardial tissue contrast in CMR imaging. While mild reactions occur in ~1% of patients, severe reactions are rare (~1 in 200,000). Older generation GBCAs can cause nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) in patients with severe renal dysfunction (eGFR <30 mL/min per 1.73 m²), but newer macrocyclic-structured GBCAs have an excellent safety profile even in chronic kidney disease. Routine pretest eGFR screening is no longer recommended for group II agents.
keyPoints: Mild reactions from GBCAs occur in ~1% of patients
Severe reactions are rare at approximately one in 200,000 patients
NSF risk with older generation GBCAs in eGFR <30 mL/min per 1.73 m²
Group II GBCAs safe for use even in severe renal dysfunction
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CONTRAST AGENTS

items.subtopic: SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: MRI Safety in Patients with Cardiac Devices
items.content: Patients with FDA-approved MRI-conditional cardiac devices can safely undergo MRI studies up to 3T under specific conditions. Non-FDA-approved devices may also be scanned at 1.5T, provided there are no fractured or abandoned leads and experienced staff is available.
keyPoints: FDA-approved MRI-conditional devices safe for MRI up to 3T
Non-FDA-approved devices can undergo MRI at 1.5T under specific conditions
Coverage expanded by CMS since April 2018
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Diagnostic Accuracy of Cardiac Imaging for CAD
items.content: The diagnostic accuracy of cardiac imaging modalities varies, with multicenter studies showing more modest results compared to single-center studies. Stress echocardiography and nuclear perfusion imaging are key tools in diagnosing and risk stratifying CAD.
keyPoints: Multicenter studies show modest diagnostic accuracies
Stress echocardiography identifies inducible wall motion abnormalities
Nuclear perfusion imaging is robust for diagnosing obstructive CAD
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Risk Stratification with Stress Echocardiography
items.content: Stress echocardiography is valuable for risk stratification in CAD. A negative result indicates a low-risk prognosis, while positive findings correlate with increased adverse event risks.
keyPoints: Negative stress echocardiogram indicates excellent prognosis
Risk of adverse events increases with severity of wall motion abnormalities
Good-quality images obtained in 85% of patients
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Role of Quantitative Myocardial Blood Flow in CAD Evaluation
items.content: Quantitative myocardial blood flow and coronary flow reserve measurements using PET can enhance the sensitivity of radionuclide imaging, especially in patients with balanced ischemia or diffuse CAD. These metrics help identify at-risk myocardium that might be missed by relative perfusion assessments alone.
keyPoints: Radionuclide imaging often misses less severe stenoses and is insensitive to multivessel disease extent.
Quantitative myocardial blood flow and coronary flow reserve with PET can mitigate these limitations.
A normal coronary flow reserve has a high negative predictive value for excluding high-risk angiographic CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Utility of Hybrid CT and Nuclear Perfusion Imaging
items.content: Hybrid imaging techniques, such as SPECT/CT or PET/CT, integrate myocardial scar and ischemia assessment with coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring. This dual-modality approach enhances risk stratification by combining perfusion data with anatomic atherosclerosis extent.
keyPoints: Integration of CT and gamma camera allows simultaneous myocardial and CAC evaluation.
Perfusion imaging identifies obstructive atherosclerosis, while CAC scores quantify anatomical atherosclerosis extent.
This hybrid approach improves risk assessment models beyond nuclear imaging alone.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Interpreting Cardiac CT Angiography Results
items.content: Cardiac CTA is highly sensitive for excluding CAD, particularly in patients with low-intermediate clinical risk presenting with chest pain. However, its specificity decreases in the presence of high coronary calcium scores due to blooming artifacts. The addition of stress myocardial perfusion CT or $FFR_{CT}$ can help determine the hemodynamic significance of stenoses.
keyPoints: Cardiac CTA has a high negative predictive value for excluding obstructive CAD.
High CAC scores (>400) reduce specificity due to calcium blooming artifacts.
$FFR_{CT}$ is emerging as a tool to assess the hemodynamic significance of stenoses.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: CMR Imaging for CAD: Advantages and Risks
items.content: Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance (CMR) imaging is used to evaluate ischemia from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) by assessing regional myocardial perfusion or wall motion at rest and during pharmacologic stress. It offers higher spatial resolution compared to SPECT, allowing detection of subendocardial ischemia or infarction that may be missed by SPECT.
keyPoints: CMR uses GBCA bolus for myocardial perfusion imaging; relative perfusion deficits appear as low signal intensity (black) within the myocardium.
Late Gadolinium Enhancement (LGE) detects bright areas of myocardial scar, allowing quantification of ischemia.
Dobutamine CMR has better diagnostic accuracy than dobutamine echocardiography for CAD detection, especially in patients with poor acoustic windows.
High-dose dobutamine carries a ~1% risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, preventable with proper monitoring.
Stress perfusion CMR provides robust risk stratification and guides invasive investigation and coronary revascularization.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Imaging Strategy for Patients without Known CAD
items.content: For patients with suspected CAD presenting with chest pain, stress imaging testing is recommended. CMR and PET have higher test accuracy than SPECT. In intermediate-risk patients, stress imaging can reclassify risk levels more accurately than exercise treadmill testing (ETT). ETT remains valuable for those with a normal or nearly normal resting ECG who can exercise.
keyPoints: 2021 ACC/AHA guidelines recommend class I stress imaging testing and coronary CTA for symptomatic patients without known CAD.
CMR and PET show higher test accuracy than SPECT in diagnosing CAD.
Stress imaging with SPECT or echocardiography can reclassify intermediate-risk patients as low or high risk, more accurately than ETT alone.
ETT is effective for initial diagnosis in symptomatic, low-risk women who can exercise, showing similar 2-year outcomes to stress radionuclide imaging but at lower costs.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Exercise Testing Challenges and Alternatives in Women
items.content: Exercise testing for diagnosing obstructive CAD in women presents challenges due to lower prevalence of obstructive CAD, differences in test accuracy, and higher false-positive rates. Stress imaging may be preferred over standard stress testing in such cases.
keyPoints: Women have a lower pretest probability of obstructive CAD, leading to more false positives in exercise tests.
Microvascular coronary artery dysfunction can cause true myocardial ischemia detected by positive ETT in women.
Recent data suggest that for symptomatic, low-risk women who can exercise, standard ETT is effective and cost-efficient compared to stress radionuclide imaging.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Role of Coronary CTA in Patients with Intermediate-High Likelihood of CAD
items.content: Coronary CTA is useful for patients with intermediate-high likelihood of CAD, such as diabetics or those with renal impairment. It helps exclude obstructive CAD and informs management decisions, especially when pretest risk is low.
keyPoints: Increased overall sensitivity for diagnosis of CAD in specific populations (e.g., diabetics, renal impairment).
Effective exclusion of obstructive CAD in patients with atypical chest pain and low-pretest risk.
In patients with intermediate or higher pretest risk, coronary CTA is less effective due to limited accuracy in defining stenosis severity and predicting ischemia.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Use of Stress Imaging in Known CAD Patients
items.content: In patients with known CAD, stress imaging is preferred over exercise ECG due to its ability to localize and quantify ischemia, aiding in targeted revascularization decisions.
keyPoints: Exercise ECG has limitations post-myocardial infarction or revascularization.
Stress imaging localizes and quantifies the magnitude of ischemia.
Normal stress imaging identifies low-risk patients; abnormal results relate to increased posttest risk.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Evaluation Strategies for Acute Chest Pain in the ED
items.content: The primary goal of evaluating acute chest pain in the emergency department is to exclude ACS. Stress imaging and novel biomarkers aid in risk stratification and diagnosing conditions mimicking ACS.
keyPoints: ACS represents a small minority of acute chest pain cases in the ED.
Serial ECGs and cardiac biomarkers are used for ruling out ACS.
Stress echocardiography, radionuclide imaging, and CMR can diagnose conditions that mimic ACS (e.g., myocarditis, takotsubo cardiomyopathy).
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.title: Role of Coronary CTA in Acute Chest Pain Management
items.content: Coronary CT angiography (CTA) is a rapid and accurate method for excluding coronary artery disease (CAD) in patients with acute chest pain. It has been shown to be feasible, safe, and accurate compared to usual care, which often involves stress imaging.
keyPoints: Four randomized clinical trials demonstrated the safety and accuracy of coronary CTA in the ED.
Patients had a very low clinical risk; no deaths or significant myocardial infarctions were observed.
Coronary CTA resulted in decreased length of stay and potential cost savings.
More patients assigned to coronary CTA underwent cardiac catheterization (6.3% vs 8.4%) and revascularization (2.6% vs 4.6%) compared to usual care.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 5)

items.title: Triage of Patients with Acute Chest Pain
items.content: Not all patients presenting with acute chest pain require specialized imaging. Those with very low clinical risk and negative biomarkers, particularly high-sensitivity troponin assays, can be safely triaged without further imaging.
keyPoints: Patients with very low clinical risk and negative biomarkers can be safely triaged without specialized imaging.
High-sensitivity troponin assays are crucial in assessing the need for further imaging.
Imaging tests should be carefully considered for patients with low-intermediate risk due to trade-offs.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 5)

items.title: Echocardiography as the First-Line Imaging for Valvular Heart Disease
items.content: Echocardiography is generally considered the first imaging test for assessing valvular heart disease due to its cost-effectiveness and comprehensive capability in evaluating both regurgitant and stenotic lesions.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is the most cost-effective screening method for valvular heart disease.
Indications include cardiac murmurs, symptoms of breathlessness, syncope or presyncope, and preoperative exams.
A standard echocardiographic exam should qualitatively and quantitatively assess all valves.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Assessment of Aortic Stenosis Severity
items.content: The severity of aortic stenosis is assessed by estimating the pressure gradient across the valve and the valve area, with specific thresholds indicating different levels of severity.
keyPoints: Peak instantaneous velocities >3.0 m/s indicate moderate or higher aortic stenosis.
Pressure gradients corresponding to these velocities are 36 mmHg for 3.0 m/s and 64 mmHg for 4.0 m/s.
Aortic valve areas <1.0 cm² are considered severe, and <0.6 cm² are critical.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Challenges in Low-Flow or Low-Gradient Aortic Stenosis
items.content: In cases of low-flow or low-gradient aortic stenosis, distinguishing whether the valve can open further requires careful assessment and sometimes provocative testing.
keyPoints: Low-flow or low-gradient aortic stenosis may require dobutamine echocardiography for accurate assessment.
It is crucial to determine if the valve behaves like a stenotic valve due to low-pressure gradient.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Role of Imaging in Transcatheter Aortic Valve Intervention
items.content: Imaging is crucial for preprocedural planning, intraprocedural optimization, and follow-up in transcatheter aortic valve interventions. CT defines access site eligibility and anatomical relationships, while transesophageal echocardiography ensures proper device sizing and function during implantation.
keyPoints: CTA of the aorta and iliac arteries is used for defining access site eligibility.
Cardiac CT and transesophageal echocardiography define device size.
Transesophageal echocardiography assesses prosthesis position, function, and immediate complications during implantation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Assessment Criteria for Aortic Regurgitation
items.content: Aortic regurgitation assessment involves evaluating valve structure and function. Serial assessments of ventricular size and function are necessary, with critical thresholds being an end-systolic diameter >5.5 cm or LVEF below normal indicating higher risk.
keyPoints: Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital abnormality causing aortic regurgitation.
Aortic root dilatation should be measured and monitored in patients with aortic regurgitation.
End-systolic diameter >5.5 cm or LVEF below normal indicates higher risk of death or heart failure.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Advantages of CMR in Aortic Regurgitation Assessment
items.content: CMR provides more accurate assessments than echocardiography for small changes in cardiac size or function and better quantification of aortic regurgitant volume. It is often used for serial follow-up due to its 3D imaging capabilities.
keyPoints: CMR is more accurate than echocardiography for longitudinal assessment of cardiac size/function.
CMR accurately quantifies aortic regurgitant volume better than echocardiography.
3D CMR imaging helps determine the etiology and monitor progression of aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Quantitative Assessment of Mitral Regurgitation
items.content: For clinical decisions about surgical intervention in mitral regurgitation, quantitative assessment is recommended over visual estimation due to potential inaccuracies. The PISA method is commonly used for this purpose.
keyPoints: Visual estimation can under- or overestimate severity, especially with eccentric jets.
Quantitative assessment is crucial for clinical decisions about surgery.
The PISA method estimates flow acceleration velocity proximal to the valve.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Etiology and Treatment of Mitral Regurgitation
items.content: Understanding whether mitral regurgitation is intrinsic or functional is vital for surgical planning. Functional mitral regurgitation often results from left ventricular dilatation, affecting treatment approaches.
keyPoints: Functional mitral regurgitation is due to apical displacement of papillary muscles in a dilated ventricle.
Intrinsic valve abnormalities differ from functional causes in surgical or procedural treatments.
Etiology knowledge aids surgeons in planning mitral valve surgery.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Assessment of Mitral Stenosis
items.content: Rheumatic disease is the most common cause of mitral stenosis, characterized by specific leaflet deformations. Echocardiography helps determine etiology and assess severity through valve areas, gradients, and impacts on cardiac structures.
keyPoints: Rheumatic mitral stenosis shows tethering at leaflet tips with a hockey stick-type deformation.
Echocardiography estimates valve areas and gradients to assess severity.
Assessment of left atrial size and right ventricular function helps determine the impact of mitral stenosis.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.title: Role of LGE Imaging in Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Late Gadolinium Enhancement (LGE) imaging by Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR) is the most effective method for detecting and assessing the extent of infarcted myocardium. It accurately identifies both acute and chronic infarcts with high sensitivity.
keyPoints: LGE CMR has a sensitivity of 99% for acute infarcts and 94% for chronic infarcts.
Regions of microvascular obstruction can be visualized as dense hypoenhanced areas within the bright region of infarction.
Both LGE presence and microvascular obstruction indicate increased clinical risk.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.title: Assessment of Mechanical Complications Post-Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Echocardiography is the preferred method for evaluating mechanical complications following a myocardial infarction, such as mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle dysfunction or rupture, ventricular septal defects, and cardiac ruptures.
keyPoints: A new severe systolic murmur may indicate severe mitral regurgitation or a ventricular septal defect.
Early diagnosis and surgical treatment of contained ruptures (pseudoaneurysms) are crucial for survival.
Thrombus within the pericardial space post-myocardial infarction suggests myocardial rupture, requiring immediate surgical intervention.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.title: Imaging for Residual Ischemia and Viability Post-Myocardial Infarction
items.content: In cases of acute or subacute myocardial infarction, assessing residual ischemia and viability is important, especially in patients with recurrent symptoms. Various imaging techniques provide insights into myocardial viability and ischemia.
keyPoints: Radionuclide imaging, particularly PET, has a high sensitivity and negative predictive value for detecting myocardial viability.
Dobutamine echocardiography offers higher specificity and positive predictive accuracy compared to radionuclide methods.
CMR provides similar predictive accuracies as dobutamine echocardiography.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.title: Role of CMR in Differentiating Heart Failure Etiologies
items.content: Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR) is instrumental in differentiating heart failure etiologies by providing detailed information on myocardial ischemia, scar patterns, and tissue characteristics. It helps distinguish between infarction and other cardiomyopathies based on the pattern of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE).
keyPoints: CMR quantifies left and right ventricular volumes and function.
Pattern of LGE differentiates infarction (subendocardial, coronary territory) from infiltrative/inflammatory cardiomyopathies (mid/subepicardial, non-coronary distribution).
T1 or T2 tissue mapping assesses myocardial edema to determine chronicity of acute coronary syndromes or noncoronary inflammatory conditions.
T2* mapping evaluates myocardial iron deposition for cardiac toxicity assessment.
Infiltrative cardiomyopathy like amyloidosis shows biventricular increased wall thickness and bilateral atrial enlargement, with a characteristic pattern of diffuse endocardial infiltration.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 2)

items.title: Utilizing PET Imaging in Inflammatory Cardiomyopathies
items.content: PET imaging plays a complementary role in evaluating inflammatory cardiomyopathies by identifying areas of active inflammation through glucose uptake patterns. It is particularly useful for monitoring therapeutic response in conditions like sarcoidosis during immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: PET identifies focal and/or diffuse glucose uptake to locate active inflammation, especially in sarcoidosis.
Used to monitor therapeutic response in patients undergoing immunosuppressive therapy.
Radionuclide imaging, including PET, quantifies myocardial ischemia and viability for decision-making related to myocardial revascularization.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT
items.title: Criteria for Diagnosing Cardiotoxicity in Cancer Patients
items.content: Cardiotoxicity is a significant concern in cancer treatment due to its potential to negate early survival benefits. The diagnosis hinges on specific changes in left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), with clinical symptoms guiding the threshold.
keyPoints: A >5% reduction in LVEF to <55% with heart failure symptoms indicates cardiotoxicity.
An asymptomatic patient showing a >10% drop in LVEF to <55% also meets diagnostic criteria for cardiotoxicity.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT

items.subtopic: ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT
items.title: Role of Noninvasive Imaging in Monitoring Cardiac Toxicity
items.content: Noninvasive imaging is crucial for diagnosing and monitoring cardiac toxicity during cancer treatment. While radionuclide angiography has been traditionally preferred, echocardiography is increasingly important.
keyPoints: Radionuclide angiography has long been the standard technique for assessing cardiac function in this context.
Echocardiography is now a major tool for evaluating and monitoring cardiotoxicity.
Novel imaging techniques like deformation imaging with echocardiography and fibrosis imaging with CMR show promise but are still experimental.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT

items.title: Echocardiography as the Initial Test for Pericardial Disease
items.content: Echocardiography is the preferred initial diagnostic tool for assessing pericardial disease, particularly effusions. It helps determine the location, size, and physiological impact of accumulated fluid.
keyPoints: Initial test of choice for pericardial disease assessment.
Can evaluate pericardial constrictive physiology.
Significant effusion is considered when distance from parietal to visceral layer exceeds ~1 cm.
Features suggestive of tamponade include diastolic collapse of the right ventricular free wall and respiratory flow variation on Doppler.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Pericardial Tamponade
items.content: Pericardial tamponade is diagnosed when intrapericardial pressure exceeds heart filling pressures, often the right ventricle. Echocardiography aids in diagnosis by identifying diastolic collapse and respiratory flow variation.
keyPoints: Tamponade occurs when intrapericardial pressure surpasses ventricular filling pressures.
Rapid fluid accumulation can cause tamponade without large effusions.
Diagnosis involves clinical assessment, including blood pressure with pulsus paradoxus.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.title: Assessment of Pericardial Constriction
items.content: Chronic pericarditis can lead to pericardial constriction, impairing diastolic filling. Diagnosis requires assessing signs and symptoms alongside imaging, as some patients may not show significant thickening.
keyPoints: Constriction results from chronic inflammation leading to thickening and calcification.
Approximately one in five patients with severe constriction shows no significant thickening on imaging or surgery.
Doppler echocardiography reveals marked respiratory changes in diastolic flow.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.title: Echocardiography as the First-Line Modality for Detecting Cardiac Masses
items.content: Echocardiography is typically the first imaging modality to detect cardiac masses, which can include thrombus, tumor, or vegetation. It provides initial visualization and helps guide further diagnostic evaluation.
keyPoints: Echocardiography detects cardiac mass initially.
Differential diagnoses for a cardiac mass include thrombus, tumor, or vegetation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC THROMBUS AND MASS

items.title: CMR and CT in Characterizing Cardiac Masses
items.content: Cardiac MRI (CMR) and CT are used to further characterize cardiac masses detected by echocardiography. CMR offers higher tissue contrast differentiation, robust cine imaging, and multifaceted techniques within a single session to determine the physiologic characteristics of the mass.
keyPoints: CMR provides higher tissue contrast differentiation than CT.
CMR allows for robust cine imaging and multifaceted techniques in one session.
Gadolinium contrast enhancement patterns can detect vascularity within a mass, differentiating tumors from thrombi.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC THROMBUS AND MASS

items.subtopic: ROLE OF IMAGING IN INFECTIOUS AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE
items.title: Echocardiography in Suspected Endocarditis: Importance and Limitations
items.content: In patients with suspected endocarditis, echocardiography is crucial for identifying vegetations or intramycardial abscesses. While transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is preferred, especially in those with prosthetic valves, the absence of visible vegetations does not rule out endocarditis due to potential limitations in imaging resolution.
keyPoints: Echocardiography identifies vegetations and abscesses; TEE is preferred for patients with prosthetic valves.
Absence of vegetation on echocardiography does not exclude endocarditis.
Vegetation size has prognostic importance and can influence surgical decisions.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ROLE OF IMAGING IN INFECTIOUS AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE | EVALUATION OF COMMON CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE ADULT

items.subtopic: ROLE OF IMAGING IN INFECTIOUS AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE
items.title: PET Imaging in Endocarditis: Emerging Role
items.content: PET metabolic imaging, particularly with $^{18}$F-FDG, is emerging as a useful technique to identify infection sources in patients with prosthetic valves or implantable devices when echocardiography and blood cultures are negative. It also aids in monitoring vascular inflammation and response to immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: PET imaging with $^{18}$F-FDG is useful for identifying infections, especially when other tests are negative.
It helps monitor vascular inflammation and treatment responses.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ROLE OF IMAGING IN INFECTIOUS AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE | EVALUATION OF COMMON CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE ADULT

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF COMMON CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE ADULT
items.title: Diagnosing Patent Foramen Ovale (PFO) in Adults
items.content: In adults, PFO is identified using an agitated saline bubble study rather than color flow Doppler due to the one-way flap mechanism. This method is particularly important for patients with stroke or systemic embolus of unknown etiology.
keyPoints: PFO can be present in up to 25% of adults and may lead to paradoxical emboli.
Agitated saline bubble study, not color flow Doppler, is the preferred diagnostic method.
Maneuvers like Valsalva or sniffing increase right atrial pressure to detect PFO.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ROLE OF IMAGING IN INFECTIOUS AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE | EVALUATION OF COMMON CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE ADULT

items.title: AI Enhances Image Acquisition in Cardiac Imaging
items.content: Artificial intelligence (AI) significantly improves image acquisition processes in cardiac imaging, particularly in echocardiography and MRI. AI software aids in guiding users to obtain optimal images during echocardiography by directing the manual application of probes. In cardiac MRI, AI reduces complexities associated with image acquisition and reconstruction, thus enhancing patient throughput and image quality.
keyPoints: AI directs optimal image acquisition in echocardiography
Reduces complexity in cardiac MRI scanning procedures
Improves patient throughput and image quality
FDA-approved commercial AI solutions available for cardiac MRI
Cardiac MRI scan time typically 45–60 minutes
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN CARDIAC IMAGING

items.title: AI in Postprocessing and Risk Prediction in Cardiac Imaging
items.content: Artificial intelligence is integrated into postprocessing tasks such as image segmentation, routine measurements of cardiac dimensions/volumes, and function using echocardiography and CMR. AI also quantifies coronary artery calcium (CAC) on nongated CT images and plaque burden on coronary CTA. Emerging evidence suggests that AI can refine risk prediction by incorporating imaging markers.
keyPoints: AI used for image segmentation and routine cardiac measurements
Quantifies CAC on nongated CT images like PET/CT and SPECT/CT
Assesses plaque burden on coronary CTA
Potential to refine risk prediction using imaging markers
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN CARDIAC IMAGING

items.explanation: The FDA-approved use is for opacification of left-sided chambers and to improve delineation of the left ventricular endocardial border in patients with suboptimal echocardiograms.

items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding MRI studies in patients with FDA-approved cardiac devices?
options: MRI studies are safe up to 3T under well-defined scanning conditions.
MRI studies can only be performed at 1.5T for FDA-approved devices.
FDA-approved devices require no special conditions for MRI studies.
These devices cannot undergo any form of MRI study.
items.explanation: FDA-approved MRI-conditional internal cardiac defibrillators and pacemakers can safely undergo an MRI study up to 3T under well-defined scanning conditions in experienced centers.

items.question: Under what conditions can MRI studies be safely performed in patients with non-FDA-approved cardiac devices?
options: At any field strength without restrictions.
At 1.5T under normal operational settings and other specified conditions.
items.explanation: For non-FDA-approved cardiac devices, MRI studies can be safely performed at 1.5T under normal operational settings when there are no fractured, epicardial, or abandoned leads and experienced staff is available to interrogate the device before and after the study.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: When did the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services expand coverage of MRI studies in patients with an implanted legacy device?
items.explanation: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services expanded coverage of MRI studies in patients with an implanted legacy device since April 2018.

items.question: What is the pretest probability of CAD dependent on in symptomatic patients?
options: Type of symptom, age, gender, and coronary risk factors.
It is not dependent on any specific factor.
items.explanation: In symptomatic patients, the pretest probability of CAD differs based on the type of symptom (typical angina, atypical angina, noncardiac chest pain), as well as age, gender, and coronary risk factors.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS OF CMR IN PATIENTS WITH PACEMAKERS AND DEFIBRILLATORS | CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is a recognized limitation of radionuclide imaging in the evaluation of coronary artery disease?
options: It often uncovers only coronary territories supplied by the most severe stenoses.
It provides detailed information about nonobstructive atherosclerosis.
It has high sensitivity for multivessel disease.
It accurately delineates the extent of obstructive angiographic CAD.
items.explanation: Radionuclide imaging often uncovers only coronary territories supplied by the most severe stenoses, making it relatively insensitive to accurately delineate the extent of obstructive angiographic CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is associated with a very high negative predictive value for excluding high-risk angiographic CAD?
items.explanation: A normal coronary flow reserve is associated with a very high negative predictive value for excluding high-risk angiographic CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the implication of a CAC score >400?
options: It indicates a low likelihood of obstructive CAD.
It suggests a higher likelihood of obstructive CAD.
It confirms the absence of coronary artery disease.
It is not predictive of any form of coronary artery disease.
items.explanation: High CAC scores, such as an Agatston score >400, are generally predictive of a higher likelihood of obstructive CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the limitation of coronary CT angiography in patients with high coronary calcium scores?
options: It has low sensitivity for detecting CAD.
It cannot accurately evaluate the vessel lumen due to blooming artifact of calcium.
It provides detailed information about noncalcified plaques.
It is highly specific for identifying flow-limiting stenosis.
items.explanation: In patients with high coronary calcium scores, specificity is reduced because the blooming artifact of calcium does not allow accurate evaluation of the vessel lumen.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which pharmacologic agent is used during CMR to evaluate regional myocardial perfusion?
items.explanation: CMR evaluates for ischemia from CAD using an intravenous infusion of a vasodilator agent or dobutamine.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary advantage of stress perfusion CMR over SPECT?
items.explanation: Stress perfusion CMR has higher spatial resolution, allowing detection of subendocardial ischemia or infarction that may be missed by SPECT.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What is a potential risk associated with high-dose dobutamine during CMR?
items.explanation: High-dose dobutamine carries the risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias (~1%).
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: According to the 2021 ACC/AHA guidelines, what is recommended for symptomatic patients without prior CAD?
options: Stress imaging testing and coronary CTA
items.explanation: The guidelines assign a class I recommendation for stress imaging testing and coronary CTA in symptomatic patients without prior CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: In patients with intermediate-high likelihood of CAD, why might a coronary CTA be considered an appropriate first step?
options: It provides high accuracy in defining stenosis severity.
It has increased overall sensitivity for diagnosing CAD and improved risk stratification.
It is the most effective method to exclude obstructive CAD in all patients.
It eliminates the need for further testing in all cases.
items.explanation: Coronary CTA may be an appropriate first step due to its increased overall sensitivity for diagnosing CAD and improved risk stratification in certain patient groups.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: Why is coronary CTA less effective in patients with intermediate or higher pretest risk of CAD?
options: It has high accuracy to define stenosis severity.
It effectively excludes obstructive CAD in these patients.
It has limited accuracy to define stenosis severity and predict ischemia.
It is the preferred method for guiding revascularization decisions.
items.explanation: Coronary CTA is less effective due to its limited accuracy in defining stenosis severity and predicting ischemia in patients with intermediate or higher pretest risk of CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: What does the ISCHEMIA trial suggest about optimal medical therapy compared to an early invasive strategy?
options: Optimal medical therapy is inferior in prognostic benefit.
An early invasive strategy is always preferred.
Optimal medical therapy provides comparable prognostic benefit.
items.explanation: The ISCHEMIA trial suggests that optimal medical therapy provides comparable prognostic benefit to an early invasive strategy in patients with moderate or severe myocardial ischemia.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: Why are imaging tests preferred over standard ETT for evaluating symptomatic patients with known CAD?
options: Standard ETT provides better localization of ischemia.
Imaging tests can document the magnitude and localization of ischemia.
ETT is more effective following myocardial infarction.
Imaging tests are less accurate than standard ETT.
items.explanation: Imaging tests are preferred because they can document both the magnitude and localization of ischemia, which is important for directing therapy in patients with known CAD.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 4)

items.question: Which imaging technique is highlighted as rapid and accurate for excluding CAD in patients with acute chest pain?
items.explanation: Coronary CTA is described as a rapid and accurate technique for excluding CAD in patients with acute chest pain.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: What was the observed outcome regarding deaths and myocardial infarctions between groups receiving coronary CTA and usual care?
options: There were more deaths in the coronary CTA group
There were fewer myocardial infarctions without differences between groups
There were significant differences in postdischarge ED visits
items.explanation: The studies showed no deaths and very few myocardial infarctions with no differences between the coronary CTA group and usual care.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: What was a noted observation from a recent meta-analysis comparing coronary CTA to usual care?
options: More patients underwent cardiac catheterization with usual care
Cost savings were reported in all studies
There were more revascularizations after stress imaging testing strategies
Coronary CTA led to fewer referrals for cardiac catheterization
items.explanation: The meta-analysis observed that more patients assigned to coronary CTA underwent cardiac catheterization compared to usual care.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (part 5)

items.question: Which imaging modality is generally considered the first test for assessing valvular heart disease?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is generally considered the first imaging test for the assessment of valvular heart disease.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common congenital anomaly associated with aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: The most common congenital anomaly associated with aortic stenosis is the bicuspid aortic valve.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What technique is considered the most accurate for assessing the severity of aortic stenosis in patients with severe left ventricular dysfunction?
options: Continuity principle to estimate valve area
items.explanation: The continuity principle is the most accurate technique for assessing the severity of aortic stenosis, especially in patients with severe left ventricular dysfunction.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which condition can be easily distinguished from aortic stenosis on echocardiography due to visible opening of valve leaflets during systole?
items.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy can be easily distinguished from aortic stenosis on echocardiography as the valve leaflets can be seen opening during systole.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered the imaging of choice for long-term surveillance in patients with aortic regurgitation?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is the imaging modality of choice for long-term surveillance in patients with aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common congenital abnormality associated with aortic regurgitation?
items.explanation: The most common congenital abnormality associated with aortic regurgitation is the bicuspid aortic valve.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which parameter is used to decide the need for valve surgery in patients with aortic regurgitation?
options: End-systolic diameter of 5.5 cm or LVEF below normal
items.explanation: Patients whose ventricles dilate beyond an end-systolic diameter of 5.5 cm or whose LVEF declines below normal are at higher risk and may need valve surgery.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality is more accurate than echocardiography for assessing small changes in cardiac size or function longitudinally in patients with aortic insufficiency?
items.explanation: CMR is more accurate than echocardiography for assessing small changes in cardiac size or function longitudinally in patients with aortic insufficiency.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which method is generally used for quantitative assessment of the severity of mitral regurgitation?
options: Visual estimation of color flow Doppler
Assessment of ventricular structure and function
items.explanation: The PISA method is used for quantitative assessment of mitral regurgitation severity.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What can functional mitral regurgitation be secondary to?
options: Dilatation and remodeling of the left ventricle
items.explanation: Functional mitral regurgitation is often secondary to dilatation and remodeling of the left ventricle.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What does CMR help evaluate in patients with inadequate echocardiographic assessment?
options: Estimation of valve areas and gradients
items.explanation: CMR can directly quantify regurgitant volume in mitral regurgitation when echocardiography is inadequate.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What characterizes rheumatic mitral stenosis on echocardiography?
options: Tethering at the leaflet tips and pliability
items.explanation: Rheumatic mitral stenosis is characterized by tethering at the leaflet tips and relative pliability.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which imaging technique is considered the best for identifying infarct location after myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: LGE imaging by CMR is the best technique for identifying infarct location after myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.question: What is a common early post-myocardial infarction phenomenon that can lead to misleading ejection fraction estimates?
items.explanation: Myocardial stunning is common in the early post-myocardial infarction period, especially after reperfusion therapy.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.question: What imaging technique is best for assessing suspected mechanical complications after myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is the best method for assessing suspected mechanical complications after myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique has a higher negative predictive value than dobutamine echocardiography for residual ischemia and/or viability?
items.explanation: Radionuclide imaging, especially PET, is highly sensitive with a higher negative predictive value than dobutamine echocardiography.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality can differentiate infarction from other forms of cardiomyopathies based on the pattern of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE)?
items.explanation: Multiparametric CMR can differentiate infarction from other cardiomyopathies based on the pattern of LGE.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 2)

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of cardiac amyloidosis observed in strain echocardiography?
items.explanation: Strain echocardiography shows apical sparing of systolic function in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging technique is used to distinguish between transthyretin and light chain cardiac amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Bone scintigraphy is used to distinguish between transthyretin and light chain cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION AND HEART FAILURE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT
items.question: What is the accepted standard for clinical diagnosis of cardiotoxicity in patients undergoing cancer treatment?
options: >5% reduction in LVEF to <55% with symptoms of heart failure, or a >10% drop in LVEF to <55% in asymptomatic patients
>10% increase in LVEF to >60% with symptoms of heart failure
<5% reduction in LVEF to <50% without symptoms
No change in LVEF but presence of arrhythmias
items.explanation: The standard is defined as a >5% reduction in LVEF to <55% with heart failure symptoms, or a >10% drop to <55% in asymptomatic patients.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: Which imaging technique has been the traditional choice for diagnosing cardiac toxicity in cancer treatment?
items.explanation: Radionuclide angiography has been the traditional technique of choice for diagnosing cardiac toxicity.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: Which novel imaging approaches are being advocated for assessing cardiac function in cancer patients?
options: Deformation imaging with echocardiography and fibrosis imaging with CMR
items.explanation: Novel approaches include deformation imaging with echocardiography and fibrosis imaging with CMR.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · ASSESSING CARDIAC FUNCTION IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered the initial test of choice for assessing pericardial disease, especially effusions?
items.explanation: Echocardiography remains the initial test of choice for assessing pericardial disease.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.question: What is a significant finding on echocardiography that suggests pericardial tamponade?
options: Diastolic collapse of the left ventricular free wall
Systolic expansion of the right atrium
Doppler evidence of respiratory flow variation
items.explanation: Doppler evidence of respiratory flow variation is suggestive of pericardial tamponade.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.question: What can lead to the accumulation of pericardial fluid besides inflammation?
items.explanation: Other reasons for accumulation of pericardial fluid include infection, malignancy, and bleeding into the pericardium.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.question: What is a key factor in determining the presence of pericardial tamponade?
options: Intrapericardial pressure exceeding ventricular filling pressures
items.explanation: The balance between intrapericardial pressure and ventricular pressure is more important than the extent of fluid accumulation.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · PERICARDIAL DISEASE

items.question: Which imaging modality is most advantageous for assessing the tissue contrast differentiation of a cardiac mass?
items.explanation: CMR has higher tissue contrast differentiation compared to other modalities.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC THROMBUS AND MASS

items.question: What imaging characteristic helps differentiate a thrombus from a tumor on LGE imaging?
items.explanation: A mural thrombus often has a characteristic 'etched' appearance on LGE imaging.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC THROMBUS AND MASS

items.question: Which condition is more common than primary cardiac tumors?
items.explanation: Metastatic cardiac malignancy is about 20-fold more common than primary cardiac tumors.
items.reference: Noninvasive Cardiac Imaging Echocardiography Nuclear Cardiology and Ma · CARDIAC THROMBUS AND MASS

items.question: How can vegetations be visualized in patients with suspected endocarditis?
options: Using high temporal resolution imaging techniques like echocardiography
By measuring the size and location of a vegetation

options: Improves patient throughput and image quality
Limits availability of experienced scanning operators

items.statement: Point-of-care/Handheld Echocardiography is typically used by cardiologists only.

items.statement: Myocardial strain imaging can be assessed using both Doppler and 2D echocardiography.

items.statement: Thallium-201 is commonly used in contemporary myocardial perfusion imaging.

items.statement: PET radiopharmaceuticals like rubidium-82 have a short half-life, making them suitable for pharmacologic stress imaging.

items.statement: CT calcium scoring requires the administration of iodinated contrast agents.

items.statement: Coronary CT angiography can be enhanced by using sublingual nitroglycerin.

items.statement: T2 mapping is routinely used to quantify myocardial tissue characteristics, specifically for evaluating myocardial edema.

items.statement: Vector ECG-gating and repetitive patient breath-holding are no longer necessary due to technical advancements.
